core_takeaway: Agent_1 spoke first and successfully normalized Pencil staying with Agent_1, but that anchor only secured a 41-point floor. Agent_3, speaking last in each discussion cycle, clearly steered the group toward Option A, and Agent_1 ultimately proposed the allocation that gave Agent_3 Jewel+Stone+Quill for 90 utility.
why_or_counterexample: This is a counterexample to first-position advantage. The first speaker's anchor became accepted, but it was too narrow: it protected only Pencil while leaving the high-value three-item bundle to Agent_3. The accepted substance favored the last position, and the raw votes confirm Agent_3 accepted Proposal 1 because it was their highest immediate utility.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by asking to lock Pencil and invited a clean three-way deal. Agent_2 and Agent_3 accepted the Pencil constraint, but Agent_3 publicly identified Option A as best because it delivered Quill, Stone, and Jewel. Agent_1 briefly pushed Option B to add Jewel for self, but Agent_2 and Agent_3 kept returning to Option A.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All three private-thinking entries converged on a top-item fairness frame: Pencil to Agent_1, Apple to Agent_2, Quill+Stone to Agent_3, with Jewel flexible. That frame made Option A look low-conflict even though it gave Agent_3 the largest utility.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Agent_1 formally proposed Option A, allocating only Pencil to self and Jewel+Stone+Quill to Agent_3. Agent_2 proposed the more favorable Agent_1 Option B. Agent_3's own proposal was self-harming and rejected in raw votes, so the final result came from Agent_1's proposal rather than Agent_3's formal proposal.
phase_notes.private_voting: Raw votes show Proposal 1 accepted by all three, while Proposal 2 was rejected by Agent_3 and Proposal 3 was rejected by Agent_1 and Agent_3. The tabulation text has an internal mismatch: it lists Proposal #1 as 3 accept, 0 reject, but then says Proposal #3 was accepted; the final allocation matches Proposal #1.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection section is present in the transcript.

mechanisms.theme: Early anchor protected only Agent_1's floor
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the first turn to make Pencil the central constraint, and the others largely accepted that frame. This is a limited first-position benefit, but it did not extend to Jewel or to overall payoff dominance.

mechanisms.theme: Last speaker converted the frame into Option A
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3 accepted the Pencil constraint but used later discussion turns to define Option A as the clean path, because it delivered Agent_3's top two items plus Jewel.

evidence.quote: It guarantees I get my top two
I strongly prefer adopting Option A

mechanisms.theme: Private planning made Agent_3's bundle sound fair
mechanisms.explanation: The private-thinking phase repeatedly framed the deal around preserving each agent's top items. That helped justify giving Agent_3 Quill+Stone and made Jewel the flexible item, which ultimately moved to Agent_3 in the winning proposal.

evidence.quote: The most straightforward unanimous baseline is Option A

mechanisms.explanation: Although Agent_1 publicly preferred Option B at one point, the formal proposal from Agent_1 was Option A, which allocated only Pencil to Agent_1 and the three-item high-value bundle to Agent_3.

evidence.quote: Move to Option B as Round 2 baseline

mechanisms.explanation: The raw voting shows Proposal 1 had unanimous support and gave Agent_3 their highest immediate utility. Agent_3 rejected the alternative that would have given Jewel back to Agent_1.

evidence.quote: the highest immediate utility for Agent_3
